As I’ve been learning the ins-and-outs of using **Github** effectively as an independent creator over the past couple of years, one thing has become increasingly clear to me: there is **a lot more** than you might think to optimizing a **repository**. For a long time, I had assumed that you uploaded the essential files needed to make your tool or creation run successfully, filled out a few details in the web interface, maybe added a few **tags/topics** and then called it good.

I am still in the process of learning how wrong I was. 

But before we get too deep into all the ways I underestimated Github, let’s start with the fundamentals because that almost always makes the most sense. 

When stripped down to the simplest definition, Github is built upon **Git**, which is a **version control** system for tracking changes to code. Version control can be thought of as similar to system restore points on a Windows machine. In fact, lots of tools use some kind of version control, but they may not explicitly refer to it in those exact terms. 

For example, **Google Docs** creates a **Version history** of the documents you create and edit. Typically, it will collapse changes into versions where significant changes/edits showed up, so that you can **restore** a document to a **previous version** if something goes wrong.

The usefulness of a rewind button in the context of frequently edited (and often easy-to-break) online files is not difficult to glean. When you consider the unique fragility of code specifically, it becomes even sharper.

The other main idea and functionality that Github offers, which ties into the version control specifically, is **collaboration** with others on the same repo. 

The reason these two functionalities are so complementary is that almost all projects can benefit from contributions made by other developers who are interested in your project, and the version control aspect makes allowing that collaboration much lower-risk when you can revert it back to the most recent working version when problems arise.

## 

## **Connecting a Custom Domain to Github Pages**

For a long time, I didn’t do anything collaborative on Github. I mainly just used it to **host my website for free**. Maybe you just need a viable free hosting option for a static site or personal blog, or something similar. It also works great for that. 

Assuming you have already purchased a domain through a registrar like **GoDaddy** or **Namecheap**, all you have to do is add a couple key pieces of information both in your account with the registrar and Github.

1. So, for example, if you used **Namecheap** to purchase your domain, you’d login to your account there and look for **Advanced DNS** after clicking on the domain you want to host with Github Pages.

2. Once you’re there, you will need to add two different types of **DNS records**. 

3. First, you will need to create four **A records** pointing to Github’s servers.

![][image1]

| Host | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| **@** | **185.199.108.153** |
| **@** | **185.199.109.153** |
| **@** | **185.199.110.153** |
| **@** | **185.199.111.153** |

\+Add new record —\> A record —\> Host: **@** Value: **185.199.108.153**  
\+Add new record —\> A record —\> Host: **@** Value: **185.199.109.153**  
\+Add new record —\> A record —\> Host: **@** Value: **185.199.110.153**  
\+Add new record —\> A record —\> Host: **@** Value: **185.199.111.153**

| Host | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| **www** | **username.github.io.** |

4. The other type of DNS record you need to add is a **CNAME Record**.

5. \+Add new record —\> CNAME record —\> Host: **www** Value: [**username.github.io**](http://username.github.io)**.**

These two types of **DNS records** ensure that both [yourdomain.com](http://yourdomain.com) and [www.yourdomain.com](http://www.yourdomain.com) both direct visitors to your actual website on **Github Pages**.

1. Next, head over to Github and create a new **Repository**. If your domain was [yourdomain.com](http://yourdomain.com), then name the repo YourDomain.

2. Go to the repo **Settings** tab on the far right. 

3. Along the left sidebar, choose **Pages**.

4. In the text field for **Custom Domain**, enter [yourdomain.com](http://yourdomain.com) (but use your actual domain, obviously).

5. Check the box next to **Enforce HTTPS**.

Now all you have to do is wait for the DNS records to update, which can take **up to 24 hours**.

Once that happens, everything should be correctly synced up.

To test it, try visiting your domain by typing it into a browser and if it takes you to the site you created on Github, then you are all set. You can use the Github repo as your **CMS** going forward. 

## **Publishing Your Website on Github Pages**

If you’re not sure how to setup the actual website using a Github repo, it’s very simple.

You just need to have an **index.html** file that will serve as the main page that visitors will see when they type your domain in a browser.

This file can be as simple or as detailed as you want.

For example, the most bare bones version of an **index.html** file might look like this:

| \<\!DOCTYPE html\>\<html lang\="en"\>\<head\>  \<meta charset\="utf-8" /\>  \<meta name\="viewport" content\="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" /\>  \<title\>Your Domain\</title\>\</head\>\<body\>\<h1\>Welcome to my website\!\</h1\>\<p\>This site is under active development and will be live soon.\</p\>\</body\> |
| :---- |

![][image2]

While this is not anything to get too excited about, it does lay the foundation for your publishing process going forward, which is not insignificant. 

Ideally, you will either choose a template that you can modify and add your content to, or you can choose a **static site generator** if you plan to use the site for posting to a blog.

Just make sure that your **index.html** is always uploaded to, and exists, *at the root* of your Github repo.

If you want to add additional pages, that’s fine. All you need to do is create the HTML file, upload it to your repo, then link to it from your **index.html** file.

So, for example, if you wanted to add an **About** page, then you’d create a new HTML file like the following:

| \<\!DOCTYPE html\>\<html lang\="en"\>\<head\>  \<meta charset\="utf-8" /\>  \<meta name\="viewport" content\="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" /\>  \<title\>Your Domain | About\</title\>\</head\>\<body\>\<h1\>About\</h1\>\<p\>While more content is planned for the near future, this website will serve as a hub for all of my coding projects and other creative activity online. Check back soon for blog posts covering my vibe coding experiments and experimental art\!\</p\>\</body\> |
| :---- |

![][image3]  
After uploading **about.html** to your repo, you go **edit index.html** to include a link to it.

| \<\!DOCTYPE html\>\<html lang\="en"\>\<head\>  \<meta charset\="utf-8" /\>  \<meta name\="viewport" content\="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" /\>  \<title\>Your Domain\</title\>\</head\>\<body\>\<h1\>Welcome to my website\!\</h1\>\<p\>This site is under active development and will be live soon.\</p\>\<p\>To learn more about the person behind the site, visit my \<a href\="about.html"\>About\</a\> page.\</p\>\</body\> |
| :---- |

![][image4]  
You now technically have the simplest version of a **multi-page website** with linking\!  
Of course, it’s boring as hell. 

You will likely want something that has a better design than this plain text.

When evaluating **HTML templates** (free or paid) be sure that they are **responsive**, and optimized for **mobile viewing**.

Before you choose one, it’s worth pausing to consider what you want this website to be. 

* Will it serve as a **portfolio** of your digital work?

* Is it mostly going to just be a place where you can post updates to a **blog**?

* Do you plan on setting up an **ecommerce shop** to sell items?

* Will this site be an official page for a **software product**?

* Or something else entirely?

Answering this basic question first will make finding the right type of template much easier.

But even before you consider if you need a template, or figuring out which one might best suit your needs, we can add some **CSS boilerplate** to the pages we just created very easily. This type of stylistic upgrade delivers maximum impact for very little effort.

We could probably benefit from adding some better fonts for our H1 and P elements too. This is easy to do with **Google Fonts**.

Open your **index.html** and copy paste this new version in its place:

| \<\!DOCTYPE html\>\<html lang\="en"\>\<head\>  \<meta charset\="utf-8" /\>  \<meta name\="viewport" content\="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" /\>  \<title\>Abort the Supreme Court \- Privacy Policy\</title\>  \<link rel\="preconnect" href\="https://fonts.googleapis.com"\>  \<link rel\="preconnect" href\="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin\>  \<link href\="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Oswald:wght@200..700\&display=swap" rel\="stylesheet"\>\<link rel\="preconnect" href\="https://fonts.googleapis.com"\>\<link rel\="preconnect" href\="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin\>\<link href\="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto+Serif:ital,opsz,wght@0,8..144,100..900;1,8..144,100..900\&display=swap" rel\="stylesheet"\>  \<style type\="text/css"\>.container {  width: 100%;  max-width: 1200px;  margin: 0 auto;  padding: 1rem;}.grid {  display: grid;  gap: 1rem;  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr));}.flex {  display: flex;  gap: 1rem;  flex-wrap: wrap;}h1 {font-family: "Oswald", sans-serif;}p {font-family: "Roboto Serif", serif;}\</style\>\</head\>\<body\>\<div class\="container"\>\<div class\="section"\>\<h1\>WELCOME TO MY WEBSITE\</h1\>\<p\>This site is under active development and will be live soon.\</p\>\<div class\="grid"\>\</div\>\</section\>\<div class\="flex"\>\<img src\="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/monapdx/AbortOurCourt/refs/heads/main/images/designs/stigma-quote.png" width\="480"\>	\<img src\="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/monapdx/AbortOurCourt/refs/heads/main/images/designs/abortion-pills.png" width\="480"\>\<img src\="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/monapdx/AbortOurCourt/refs/heads/main/images/designs/guide-to-abortion.png" width\="480"\>\<img src\="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/monapdx/AbortOurCourt/refs/heads/main/images/designs/overturning-roe-unpopular.png" width\="480"\>\</div\>\<p\>To learn more about the person behind the site, visit my \<a href\="about.html"\>About\</a\> page.\</p\>\</div\>\</div\>\</div\>\</body\> |
| :---- |

![][image5]

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>
